original_hypothesis.title: Staking Limit Off-by-One Allows 11 NFTs Instead of 10
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The staking limit check uses `munchablesStaked[mainAccount].length > 10` instead of `>= 10`. When the array length is exactly 10 (indices 0-9 occupied), the check passes (10 > 10 = false), allowing one more push to reach 11 staked munchables. This exceeds the intended maximum of 10.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Code reads `if (munchablesStaked[mainAccount].length > 10) revert TooManyStakedMunchiesError()`. The check allows length=10 to pass, then push makes it 11. The intended behavior (suggested by error name and typical limit patterns) is max 10.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (munchablesStaked[mainAccount].length > 10) should be >= 10 to enforce max of 10

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: length > 10 allows length=10 to pass; push makes 11
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: User stakes 11 NFTs instead of intended 10, gaining ~10% extra reward capacity
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: ~10% additional schnibbles farming capacity per account compared to intended maximum

economic_questions: Does the extra slot create meaningful economic advantage?
Could users create multiple accounts to multiply the effect?
Does the uint8 loop iterator in _farmPlots handle 11 elements safely? (Yes, 11 < 255)
validation_requirements: Confirm the off-by-one: stake 10, then stake 11th successfully

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Other farmers (slight dilution of relative rewards)

validation_result.impact_summary: Users can stake 11 NFTs instead of the intended maximum of 10, gaining ~10% extra reward capacity.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The `stakeMunchable` function enforces a maximum number of staked NFTs per account using the condition `if (munchablesStaked[mainAccount].length > 10) revert TooManyStakedMunchiesError();`. When a user has exactly 10 NFTs staked, the length is 10. The condition `10 > 10` evaluates to false, allowing the execution to proceed. The function then pushes the 11th NFT into the `munchablesStaked` array. On the next attempt, the length is 11, `11 > 10` evaluates to true, and the transaction reverts. This off-by-one error allows users to stake 11 NFTs instead of the intended 10.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The code explicitly shows `> 10` instead of `>= 10` or `> 9` before pushing a new element to the array.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The check uses strictly greater than (`>`) instead of greater than or equal to (`>=`). When the array length is 10, the check passes, and a new element is pushed, resulting in a length of 11.

calling_sequence: If length is 10, the check passes
stakeMunchable pushes the new tokenId to `munchablesStaked[mainAccount]`
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: When a user with 10 staked NFTs calls `stakeMunchable`, the condition `munchablesStaked[mainAccount].length > 10` evaluates to `10 > 10`, which is false. The function proceeds to transfer the NFT and push the new `tokenId` to `munchablesStaked[mainAccount]`, increasing its length to 11.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided that affect this logic.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: A user stakes 10 NFTs normally. The user then calls `stakeMunchable` for an 11th NFT. The transaction succeeds because `10 > 10` is false. The user now has 11 NFTs staked and earns yield on all 11.
validation_result.impact_assessment: Users can bypass the intended staking limit of 10 NFTs per account, gaining an extra slot (11 total). This provides a ~10% advantage in yield generation capacity over the intended limit.
validation_result.replication_plan:
1. Create a test account and mint 11 NFTs.
2. Approve the LandManager contract to transfer the NFTs.
3. Call `stakeMunchable` 10 times with valid plot IDs.
4. Call `stakeMunchable` an 11th time. Observe that it succeeds.
5. Call `stakeMunchable` a 12th time. Observe that it reverts with `TooManyStakedMunchiesError`.
